news_summary: Breakthroughs in diabetes treatment, vision restoration, genetic health maintenance, and the benefits of fiber on sleep highlight significant advances in medical science and wellness.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées dans le traitement du diabète, la restauration de la vision, le maintien de la santé génétique et les bienfaits des fibres sur le sommeil soulignent les progrès significatifs réalisés dans les domaines de la science médicale et du bien-être.
news_summary_es: Los avances en el tratamiento de la diabetes, la restauración de la visión, el mantenimiento de la salud genética y los beneficios de la fibra en el sueño ponen de relieve los importantes avances en la ciencia médica y el bienestar.

articles.title: A bold new plan could finally cure type 1 diabetes
articles.summary: Researchers are developing a two-part therapy for type 1 diabetes: lab-made insulin-producing cells paired with custom-engineered immune cells that protect them. The goal is to stop the immune system from destroying transplanted cells — without using immunosuppressive drugs. Backed by $1 million in funding, the team hopes to create a ready-to-use treatment that could work even for people who have had diabetes for years. The approach could transform how the disease is treated.
articles.content:
At the Medical University of South Carolina (MUSC), researcher Leonardo Ferreira, Ph.D., is leading an ambitious effort to change how type 1 diabetes (T1D) is treated. Backed by $1 million from Breakthrough T1D, a leading global research and advocacy organization, Ferreira and collaborators at partner institutions are testing a new strategy aimed at treating and potentially curing the disease.

Their approach brings together stem cell science, immunology, and transplantation research. The central goal is straightforward but bold: restore insulin-producing beta cells in people with T1D without requiring immunosuppressive drugs.

"These awards support the most promising work that can significantly advance the path to cures for type 1 diabetes," said Ferreira. "This is what Breakthrough T1D believes is the next wave in type 1 diabetes therapy."

Engineering the Immune System to Protect Insulin Cells

Ferreira specializes in modifying the immune system using chimeric antigen receptors, or CARs. These engineered receptors help guide regulatory T cells, known as Tregs, to specific targets in the body. Tregs play an essential role in keeping immune responses under control and preventing excessive damage, including the autoimmune attack seen in T1D. In simple terms, they act like bodyguards, preventing the immune system from going too far and harming healthy tissue.

He is working alongside two prominent collaborators. Holger Russ, Ph.D., associate professor of Pharmacology and Therapeutics at the University of Florida, is a leader in stem cell research for T1D. Many scientists view this field as the future of transplantation because stem cells can provide a virtually unlimited supply of islet cells for research and clinical use. Michael Brehm, Ph.D., of the University of Massachusetts Medical School, completes the team. He is known for developing humanized mouse models that help researchers study human immune and metabolic responses in T1D.

What Happens in Type 1 Diabetes

Type 1 diabetes (T1D) is an autoimmune condition in which the immune system mistakenly attacks the pancreas's insulin-producing beta cells. Without these cells, the body cannot properly regulate blood sugar levels. People with T1D must closely monitor their glucose and rely on insulin injections to survive. According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, about 1.5 million Americans live with the disease. Over time, it can lead to serious complications, including nerve damage, blindness, coma, and even death.

The new Breakthrough T1D award builds on a 2021 Discovery Pilot grant from the South Carolina Clinical & Translational Research Institute (SCTR), which first brought Ferreira and Russ together. That early support laid the groundwork for this larger project, which could significantly reshape how T1D is treated.

A Two Part Cellular Therapy Strategy

In T1D, beta cells are destroyed because the immune system no longer recognizes them as part of the body. For patients with severe cases that are difficult to control with exogenous insulin, doctors can perform islet cell transplants, which include beta cells.

However, this option faces two major challenges. First, islet transplants depend on donor tissue, and there are not enough beta cells available. To address this shortage, the research team is producing its own stem cell derived islet cells in the laboratory.

The second problem is immune rejection. Transplanted beta cells, like any foreign tissue, can be attacked by the immune system. This is where Ferreira's immune engineering expertise becomes essential. Tregs naturally help calm immune responses. Ferreira modifies these cells with a CAR that recognizes a specific surface protein placed on the beta cells. This works like a GPS signal, directing the Tregs precisely to the transplanted cells.

Once there, the engineered Tregs function as targeted "bodyguards," protecting the beta cells from immune attack. The interaction works like a lock and key. When the receptor on the Treg fits the protein on the beta cell, it signals the immune system to stand down. Together, the beta cells and Tregs form a protective partnership that helps preserve insulin production after transplantation.

Avoiding Immunosuppressive Drugs

One major advantage of this combined cellular therapy is that it could eliminate the need for immunosuppressive drugs. These medications are commonly required after transplants but carry significant long term risks, especially for children.

Lab produced beta cells may also solve the long standing shortage of donor tissue. Currently, a single transplant can require beta cells from three or four donors, while most organ transplants involve a one to one match. In contrast, the team's engineered beta cells can be manufactured in the lab, frozen, and stored without losing quality. This opens the door to a scalable and reliable supply for future treatments.

The ultimate aim is to create a complete off the shelf therapy that combines engineered Tregs with lab grown beta cells. Such a treatment could be widely distributed and administered through transplantation.

"We're trying to develop a therapy that would work for all people with type 1 diabetes at every stage, even people who have had the disease for many years and have no beta cells left," said Ferreira.

Testing Durability and Long Term Impact

Moving this therapy into clinical use will require time and further research. Several questions remain, including how long the protective effects last. In preclinical studies using humanized mice, the benefits have lasted up to one month, which is the longest period studied so far. The new funding will allow researchers to explore ways to extend this protection, improve delivery methods, and determine whether multiple doses could produce longer lasting results.

By combining stem cell biology, gene editing, and immune regulation, the team is developing more than a single therapy. They are building a framework for teaching the body to repair itself. If successful, this work could eventually free patients from daily insulin injections and shift type 1 diabetes care from lifelong management to a true cure.

The implications extend beyond diabetes. Success could represent a major advance in regenerative medicine and immune based therapies.

"I think this can change how medicine is done," Ferreira said. "Instead of treating symptoms, we can actually replace the missing cells. By doing this work, we are likely to further understand how T1D starts, how it develops and how it can be treated."
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific advancement with the potential to cure type 1 diabetes by combining stem cell-derived insulin-producing cells with engineered immune cells to protect them, eliminating the need for immunosuppressive drugs. This breakthrough could transform treatment for millions of people worldwide, offering a scalable, off-the-shelf therapy that addresses both cell shortage and immune rejection. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a promising new approach with broad, meaningful impact on public health.
articles.personality_title: Scientists develop new cell therapy aiming to cure type 1 diabetes
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Type 1 diabetes (T1D) is a disease where the body's immune system attacks cells in the pancreas that make insulin. Without insulin, people must take daily shots to control their blood sugar. Current treatments cannot stop the immune system from destroying these cells.

**What happened** – Researchers at the Medical University of South Carolina, led by Dr. Leonardo Ferreira, received $1 million to develop a new two-part therapy. This therapy uses lab-grown insulin-producing cells combined with specially engineered immune cells called regulatory T cells (Tregs). These Tregs are designed to protect the transplanted insulin cells from immune attack without needing drugs that suppress the whole immune system.

**Impact** – This approach is important because it could solve two big problems: a shortage of donor cells for transplant and the immune system’s rejection of new cells. Lab-made cells can be produced in large amounts and stored for use when needed. The engineered Tregs act like bodyguards, guiding the immune system to leave the new cells alone. This means patients might avoid harmful immunosuppressive drugs and have a better chance at long-lasting insulin production.

**What’s next step** – The team will continue testing how long the protective effects last and improve the therapy’s delivery. They hope to create an off-the-shelf treatment that can help all people with T1D, even those who have had the disease for many years. If successful, this could lead to clinical trials and a new way to treat or even cure type 1 diabetes.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists are developing a promising new cell therapy that protects lab-grown insulin cells from immune attack, potentially offering a lasting cure for type 1 diabetes without harmful drugs.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques développent une nouvelle thérapie cellulaire pour guérir le diabète de type 1
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le diabète de type 1 (DT1) est une maladie où le système immunitaire attaque les cellules du pancréas qui produisent de l'insuline. Sans insuline, les personnes doivent prendre des injections quotidiennes pour contrôler leur sucre dans le sang. Les traitements actuels ne peuvent pas empêcher le système immunitaire de détruire ces cellules.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université médicale de Caroline du Sud, dirigés par le Dr Leonardo Ferreira, ont reçu 1 million de dollars pour développer une nouvelle thérapie en deux parties. Cette thérapie utilise des cellules productrices d’insuline fabriquées en laboratoire combinées à des cellules immunitaires régulatrices (Tregs) spécialement modifiées. Ces Tregs protègent les cellules transplantées sans avoir besoin de médicaments qui suppriment tout le système immunitaire.

**Impact** – Cette approche est importante car elle pourrait résoudre deux grands problèmes : la pénurie de cellules de donneurs pour la greffe et le rejet des nouvelles cellules par le système immunitaire. Les cellules fabriquées en laboratoire peuvent être produites en grande quantité et stockées. Les Tregs modifiées agissent comme des gardes du corps, guidant le système immunitaire pour qu’il épargne les nouvelles cellules. Cela signifie que les patients pourraient éviter des médicaments nocifs et conserver une production d’insuline durable.

**Prochaines étapes** – L’équipe va continuer à tester la durée de la protection et améliorer la façon d’administrer la thérapie. Ils espèrent créer un traitement prêt à l’emploi qui puisse aider toutes les personnes atteintes de DT1, même celles qui ont la maladie depuis longtemps. Si cela réussit, cela pourrait mener à des essais cliniques et à une nouvelle façon de traiter ou même guérir le diabète de type 1.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques développent une nouvelle thérapie cellulaire prometteuse qui protège les cellules d’insuline fabriquées en laboratoire contre l’attaque immunitaire, offrant potentiellement une guérison durable du diabète de type 1 sans médicaments nocifs.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos desarrollan nueva terapia celular para curar la diabetes tipo 1
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La diabetes tipo 1 (DT1) es una enfermedad en la que el sistema inmunológico ataca las células del páncreas que producen insulina. Sin insulina, las personas deben recibir inyecciones diarias para controlar el azúcar en la sangre. Los tratamientos actuales no pueden detener este ataque del sistema inmunológico.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Médica de Carolina del Sur, liderados por el Dr. Leonardo Ferreira, recibieron 1 millón de dólares para desarrollar una nueva terapia en dos partes. Esta terapia usa células productoras de insulina hechas en laboratorio junto con células inmunes especiales llamadas células T reguladoras (Tregs), diseñadas para proteger las células trasplantadas sin usar medicamentos que suprimen todo el sistema inmunológico.

**Impacto** – Este método es importante porque puede resolver dos problemas grandes: la falta de células donantes para trasplantes y el rechazo inmune de las nuevas células. Las células hechas en laboratorio se pueden producir en grandes cantidades y almacenar. Las Tregs modificadas actúan como guardianes, guiando al sistema inmunológico para que no ataque las nuevas células. Esto podría permitir que los pacientes eviten medicamentos dañinos y mantengan la producción de insulina por más tiempo.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo seguirá estudiando cuánto dura la protección y mejorará cómo administrar la terapia. Esperan crear un tratamiento listo para usar que ayude a todas las personas con DT1, incluso a quienes tienen la enfermedad desde hace años. Si funciona, podría llevar a ensayos clínicos y a una nueva forma de tratar o curar la diabetes tipo 1.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos están desarrollando una prometedora terapia celular que protege las células de insulina hechas en laboratorio del ataque inmune, ofreciendo una posible cura duradera para la diabetes tipo 1 sin medicamentos dañinos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing cluster of stylized insulin-producing beta cells gently encased and protected by softly glowing, shield-like regulatory T cells with subtle lock-and-key shaped patterns connecting them, set against a calm, abstract background symbolizing the human body’s immune system harmonizing with regenerative science.

articles.title: Wireless retinal implant helps blind patients see again
articles.summary: A tiny wireless implant is giving new hope to people blinded by advanced age-related macular degeneration. In a major international clinical trial, more than 80% of participants regained meaningful central vision, with many able to read letters and even words again after years of decline. The device replaces damaged light-sensing cells in the retina with a 2×2 mm implant that converts light into electrical signals, restoring communication between the eye and the brain.
articles.content:
A tiny wireless retinal implant has helped restore central vision in people with advanced age-related macular degeneration (AMD), according to results published in the New England Journal of Medicine. Advanced atrophic AMD, also called geographic atrophy (GA), is the most common cause of permanent blindness in older adults and affects more than 5 million people worldwide.

The international, multi-center clinical trial was co-led by José-Alain Sahel, M.D., director of the UPMC Vision Institute; Daniel Palanker, Ph.D., professor of ophthalmology at Stanford University; and Frank Holz, M.D., professor of ophthalmology at the University of Bonn, Germany.

Clinical Trial Shows Significant Vision Gains

Among the 32 participants who completed one year of follow-up, 26 (81%) experienced meaningful improvements in visual acuity. In addition, 27 participants (84%) said they were using the artificial vision provided by the device at home to read numbers or words.

On average, patients gained 25 letters on a standard eye chart, equal to about five lines, when using the implant. Overall, 81% of participants improved by at least 10 letters.

"It's the first time that any attempt at vision restoration has achieved such results in a large number of patients," said Sahel, senior author of the study and chair of the Department of Ophthalmology at the University of Pittsburgh School of Medicine. "More than 80% of the patients were able to read letters and words, and some of them are reading pages in a book. This is really something we couldn't have dreamt of when we started on this journey, together with Daniel Palanker, 15 years ago."

How the PRIMA Retinal Implant Works

As AMD advances, the central field of vision becomes blurred because the retina's light-sensing cells are permanently damaged. In a healthy eye, these photoreceptors capture light and convert it into electrical signals. Those signals travel through nerve cells at the back of the eye and then through the optic nerve to the brain, where images are formed.

The PRIMA system, originally developed by Palanker, is designed to replace damaged photoreceptors with a 2×2 mm wireless implant. The device converts light into electrical signals that stimulate surviving retinal cells. A camera built into specialized glasses records images and sends them to the implant using invisible near-infrared light. The implant then transforms that light into electrical pulses, helping restore the transmission of visual information to the brain. Users can adjust zoom and contrast settings to improve how clearly they see.

International Study Across Europe

The PRIMAvera trial enrolled 38 participants age 60 and older at 17 medical centers in five European countries: France, Germany, Italy, the Netherlands and the United Kingdom.

After 12 months of use, all side effects related to the procedure had resolved. Most participants demonstrated clear improvement in their ability to identify letters on an eye chart. One individual improved by 59 letters, equal to 12 lines.

"While we can't yet restore full 20/20 vision with the implant alone, at UPMC we are investigating methods that could further improve people's quality of life and take them above the threshold for legal blindness," Sahel said.

Regulatory Steps and Ongoing Research

Following these results, the device manufacturer, Science Corporation, has submitted applications seeking approval for clinical use in Europe and the United States. UPMC became the first U.S. center to implant the PRIMA device in 2020 in a study led by associate professor of ophthalmology Joseph Martel, M.D.

Additional researchers involved in the study represent The University of Bonn; The Adolphe de Rothschild Foundation Hospital and The 15-20 National Eye Hospital, Paris; Moorfields Eye Hospital, London; and University of Rome Tor Vergata, among others.

The study was funded by Science Corporation, Alameda, Calif., (previously Pixium Vision SA, Paris, France).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough where a wireless retinal implant restores meaningful central vision to over 80% of patients with advanced age-related macular degeneration, a leading cause of blindness worldwide. The clinical trial involved multiple international centers and demonstrated substantial, measurable improvements in vision, enabling many patients to read again after years of vision loss. This breakthrough has broad implications for millions affected by AMD, offering hope and improved quality of life.
articles.personality_title: Wireless retinal implant restores vision for people with advanced eye disease
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Age-related macular degeneration (AMD) is a common eye disease that causes blindness in older adults by damaging cells in the retina, the part of the eye that senses light. This damage blurs central vision and affects millions of people worldwide.

**What happened** – A new wireless retinal implant called PRIMA has helped people with advanced AMD regain central vision. In a large clinical trial across five European countries, 32 patients used the implant for one year. More than 80% of these participants improved their ability to see clearly, with many able to read letters and words again after years of vision loss. The implant is a tiny 2×2 mm device placed in the retina that converts light into electrical signals, sending visual information to the brain.

**Impact** – This is the first time a device has restored meaningful vision to so many patients with advanced AMD. On average, patients could read five more lines on an eye chart. Some even read whole pages in books. The implant works with special glasses that capture images and send them to the implant using invisible light, helping patients see better. This breakthrough offers new possibilities for people who have lived with severe vision loss.

**What's next step** – The company that makes the PRIMA implant is applying for approval to use it in hospitals in Europe and the United States. Researchers are also working on ways to improve the device further, aiming to help patients see even better in the future. More studies and wider use could follow once the implant is approved.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A tiny wireless implant has successfully helped over 80% of patients with advanced age-related blindness regain useful central vision, marking a major step forward in treating this common eye disease.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une prothèse rétinienne sans fil redonne la vue aux patients atteints de dégénérescence maculaire avancée
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La dégénérescence maculaire liée à l'âge (DMLA) est une maladie oculaire courante qui cause la cécité chez les personnes âgées en endommageant les cellules de la rétine, la partie de l'œil qui capte la lumière. Cette maladie trouble la vision centrale et touche des millions de personnes dans le monde.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une nouvelle prothèse rétinienne sans fil appelée PRIMA a aidé des personnes atteintes de DMLA avancée à retrouver leur vision centrale. Lors d'un grand essai clinique dans cinq pays européens, 32 patients ont utilisé l'implant pendant un an. Plus de 80 % d'entre eux ont amélioré leur capacité à voir clairement, certains pouvant lire des lettres et des mots après des années de perte de vision. L'implant est un petit dispositif de 2×2 mm placé dans la rétine, qui transforme la lumière en signaux électriques envoyés au cerveau.

**Impact** – C'est la première fois qu'un appareil restaure une vision significative à autant de patients atteints de DMLA avancée. En moyenne, les patients ont pu lire cinq lignes de plus sur une échelle de vision. Certains lisent même des pages entières de livres. L'implant fonctionne avec des lunettes spéciales qui captent les images et les transmettent à l'implant par une lumière invisible, aidant les patients à mieux voir. Cette avancée ouvre de nouvelles possibilités pour ceux qui vivent avec une perte sévère de vision.

**Prochaine étape** – L'entreprise qui fabrique l'implant PRIMA demande son autorisation pour l'utiliser dans les hôpitaux en Europe et aux États-Unis. Les chercheurs travaillent aussi à améliorer l'appareil pour que les patients voient encore mieux. D'autres études et une utilisation plus large pourraient suivre une fois l'implant approuvé.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un petit implant sans fil a permis à plus de 80 % des patients aveugles à cause de la DMLA avancée de retrouver une vision centrale utile, une avancée majeure dans le traitement de cette maladie oculaire courante.
articles.personality_title_es: Un implante retiniano inalámbrico ayuda a personas con degeneración macular avanzada a recuperar la vista
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La degeneración macular relacionada con la edad (DMAE) es una enfermedad ocular común que causa ceguera en adultos mayores al dañar las células de la retina, la parte del ojo que detecta la luz. Esta enfermedad borra la visión central y afecta a millones de personas en todo el mundo.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo implante retiniano inalámbrico llamado PRIMA ha ayudado a personas con DMAE avanzada a recuperar la visión central. En un gran ensayo clínico en cinco países europeos, 32 pacientes usaron el implante durante un año. Más del 80% mejoraron su capacidad para ver claramente, y muchos pudieron leer letras y palabras otra vez después de años sin visión. El implante es un pequeño dispositivo de 2×2 mm colocado en la retina que convierte la luz en señales eléctricas que envían información visual al cerebro.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que un dispositivo restaura visión útil a tantos pacientes con DMAE avanzada. En promedio, los pacientes pudieron leer cinco líneas más en una tabla de visión. Algunos incluso leyeron páginas completas de libros. El implante funciona con gafas especiales que capturan imágenes y las envían al implante mediante luz invisible, ayudando a los pacientes a ver mejor. Este avance abre nuevas oportunidades para quienes han vivido con pérdida grave de visión.

**Próximo paso** – La empresa que fabrica el implante PRIMA está solicitando autorización para usarlo en hospitales de Europa y Estados Unidos. Los investigadores también trabajan para mejorar el dispositivo y ayudar a los pacientes a ver aún mejor. Se esperan más estudios y un uso más amplio una vez que el implante sea aprobado.

**Frase clave** – Un pequeño implante inalámbrico ha ayudado a más del 80 % de pacientes con ceguera avanzada por DMAE a recuperar visión central útil, marcando un gran avance en el tratamiento de esta enfermedad ocular común.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a delicate, glowing retinal implant shaped like a small circuit gently nestled within an abstract eye silhouette, connected by soft beams of light to a pair of elegant glasses with a subtle camera lens, all rendered in natural, muted tones to symbolize restored vision and hope through advanced technology.

articles.title: Scientists discover the genetic switch that keeps your organs healthy
articles.summary: Scientists have uncovered a powerful genetic switch that helps some of the body’s most important immune cells grow up properly and keep our organs healthy. The switch, called MafB, guides immature precursor cells as they develop into macrophages, the body’s clean-up and repair crew that removes pathogens, clears debris, recycles iron, and supports tissue function. When MafB is missing, these cells remain stuck in an underdeveloped state and cannot fully carry out their protective roles.
articles.content:
Scientists at the University of Liège have identified a crucial genetic regulator that allows macrophages to fully mature and help maintain organ health. This regulator, called MafB, acts as a "molecular switch" that turns specific genes on or off at precise moments and in specific cells. By controlling this genetic activity, MafB enables macrophages to develop into effective defenders that support the normal function of organs throughout the body. When MafB is absent, these cells become impaired and can no longer carry out their protective responsibilities properly.

Macrophages are key immune cells found in nearly every tissue. Often described as the body's 'cleaning and maintenance team', they destroy pathogens (biological agents capable of causing disease in a host organism), clear away dead cells and debris, recycle materials such as iron, and help tissues function normally. Although macrophages tailor their behavior to the needs of each organ, they share a core identity that allows them to perform these essential tasks. Until recently, researchers did not fully understand how this shared identity is preserved across different tissues and even across species.

In research led by Professor Thomas Marichal of the Immunophysiology Laboratory (ULiège), scientists discovered that MafB, a transcription factor, serves as a central genetic switch that guides macrophages toward full functionality. As monocytes (immature precursor cells) develop into tissue macrophages, levels of MafB steadily rise, directing the maturation process. Without MafB, macrophages remain in an immature state and are unable to properly protect the tissues where they reside. "Our results show that MafB functions as a master regulator that gives macrophages their identity and equips them with the capabilities necessary to support organ health," explains immunologist Thomas Marichal. "Without this instruction programme, these cells are present but not fully operational."

Conserved Genetic Program Across Species

At the molecular level, MafB governs a broad network of genes that control critical macrophage activities, including phagocytosis (the ability to engulf harmful particles and cellular debris) and the maintenance of tissue homeostasis. The researchers found that this regulatory program is highly conserved from mice to humans and across vertebrates, emphasizing its fundamental biological importance.

The effects of losing this genetic program extend beyond immune defense alone. The team observed that disrupted macrophage maturation can impact multiple organs. Problems were seen in iron recycling within the spleen and in the normal functioning of the lungs, intestines and kidneys. These findings demonstrate how deeply macrophages contribute to the body's overall physiological balance. "These results reveal that a shared genetic programme conserved throughout evolution underlies the specialisation of macrophages across tissues," adds Domien Vanneste, first author of the scientific article. "This explains how these cells can adapt to different organs while preserving their fundamental identity."

Implications for Chronic Disease and Treatment

The discovery has important medical implications. Dysfunctional macrophages play a role in many chronic conditions, including inflammatory disorders, fibrosis, infections, and metabolic diseases. By targeting MafB or the biological pathways it regulates, researchers may be able to restore healthy macrophage function and improve tissue health across a range of diseases.

Overall, the findings establish MafB as a central and evolutionarily conserved regulator of macrophage development, identity, and function, offering new insight into how the immune system helps protect and sustain the health of multiple organs.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery identifying a genetic switch (MafB) crucial for macrophage maturation and organ health, which has broad implications for understanding and potentially treating many chronic diseases. The discovery is well-explained, with clear context and potential for wide-reaching medical benefits, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find key genetic switch that helps immune cells protect organs
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Macrophages are special immune cells found in almost every part of the body. They act like a cleaning and repair team by removing germs, clearing dead cells, recycling iron, and helping organs work well. However, scientists did not fully understand how these cells develop their important abilities.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Liège discovered a genetic switch called MafB that controls how immature cells turn into fully working macrophages. This switch turns certain genes on or off at the right time, helping macrophages grow and do their job. Without MafB, these cells stay immature and cannot protect organs properly.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that MafB is a master controller that gives macrophages their identity and power to keep organs healthy. The switch works the same way in many animals, including humans, which means it is very important for life. When MafB is missing, organs like the lungs, intestines, kidneys, and spleen do not work as well because the macrophages cannot support them.

**What’s next step** – Scientists hope to use this knowledge to develop new treatments for diseases where macrophages do not work well, like inflammation, infections, and some chronic illnesses. By targeting MafB or the genes it controls, it may be possible to help the immune system protect organs better.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The discovery of the MafB genetic switch explains how immune cells mature to protect organs and opens new paths to treat chronic diseases.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent l’interrupteur génétique clé qui aide les cellules immunitaires à protéger les organes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les macrophages sont des cellules immunitaires spéciales présentes dans presque toutes les parties du corps. Ils agissent comme une équipe de nettoyage et de réparation en éliminant les germes, en débarrassant les cellules mortes, en recyclant le fer et en aidant les organes à bien fonctionner. Cependant, les scientifiques ne comprenaient pas complètement comment ces cellules développent leurs capacités importantes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Liège ont découvert un interrupteur génétique appelé MafB qui contrôle comment les cellules immatures deviennent des macrophages pleinement fonctionnels. Cet interrupteur active ou désactive certains gènes au bon moment, aidant les macrophages à grandir et à faire leur travail. Sans MafB, ces cellules restent immatures et ne peuvent pas protéger correctement les organes.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que MafB est un contrôleur principal qui donne aux macrophages leur identité et leur pouvoir pour garder les organes en bonne santé. Cet interrupteur fonctionne de la même manière chez de nombreux animaux, y compris les humains, ce qui montre son importance pour la vie. Quand MafB manque, des organes comme les poumons, les intestins, les reins et la rate ne fonctionnent pas aussi bien parce que les macrophages ne peuvent pas les soutenir.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques espèrent utiliser cette découverte pour développer de nouveaux traitements contre les maladies où les macrophages ne fonctionnent pas bien, comme les inflammations, les infections et certaines maladies chroniques. En ciblant MafB ou les gènes qu’il contrôle, il pourrait être possible d’aider le système immunitaire à mieux protéger les organes.

**Une phrase clé** – La découverte de l’interrupteur génétique MafB explique comment les cellules immunitaires mûrissent pour protéger les organes et ouvre de nouvelles voies pour traiter les maladies chroniques.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren el interruptor genético clave que ayuda a las células inmunes a proteger los órganos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los macrófagos son células inmunes especiales que se encuentran en casi todas las partes del cuerpo. Actúan como un equipo de limpieza y reparación, eliminando gérmenes, limpiando células muertas, reciclando hierro y ayudando a que los órganos funcionen bien. Sin embargo, los científicos no entendían completamente cómo estas células desarrollan sus habilidades importantes.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Lieja descubrieron un interruptor genético llamado MafB que controla cómo las células inmaduras se convierten en macrófagos completamente funcionales. Este interruptor enciende o apaga ciertos genes en el momento correcto, ayudando a los macrófagos a crecer y hacer su trabajo. Sin MafB, estas células permanecen inmaduras y no pueden proteger bien los órganos.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que MafB es un controlador principal que da a los macrófagos su identidad y poder para mantener los órganos saludables. El interruptor funciona igual en muchos animales, incluidos los humanos, lo que significa que es muy importante para la vida. Cuando falta MafB, órganos como los pulmones, intestinos, riñones y el bazo no funcionan tan bien porque los macrófagos no pueden apoyarlos.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos esperan usar este conocimiento para crear nuevos tratamientos para enfermedades donde los macrófagos no funcionan bien, como inflamaciones, infecciones y algunas enfermedades crónicas. Al dirigir MafB o los genes que controla, podría ser posible ayudar al sistema inmunitario a proteger mejor los órganos.

**Una frase clave** – El descubrimiento del interruptor genético MafB explica cómo las células inmunes maduran para proteger los órganos y abre nuevas vías para tratar enfermedades crónicas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing, intricately branching network of cells shaped like protective guardians, with one central radiant “molecular switch” symbolized by a softly illuminated key turning within the network, surrounded by stylized organ-like forms subtly integrated into the background to represent lungs, spleen, intestines, and kidneys, all rendered in gentle natural earth tones and soft pastels.

articles.title: People who eat a lot of fibre spend more time in deep sleep
articles.summary: The most comprehensive study to date has revealed what we need to eat throughout the day to sleep well that night
articles.content:
The most comprehensive study to date has revealed what we need to eat throughout the day to sleep well that night

Want a good night’s sleep tonight? Pile your plate with plenty of fibre and plants Olga Pankova/Getty Images

Eating more fibre may help you sleep more deeply, while consuming a wider variety of fruits, vegetables and nuts could help you nod off faster. That’s according to the most comprehensive look yet at how what we eat throughout the day affects our sleep that night.

“Increasing fibre and plant variety is already recommended for overall health, carries low risk for most people and may offer sleep benefits as an additional upside,” says Hagai Rossman at the Weizmann Institute of Science in Israel.

Prior studies have linked eating fibre-rich, plant-based foods to better sleep, but these mostly relied on people recalling their diet in surveys, sometimes weeks or months later. In addition, when sleep has been objectively measured, it has typically been via movement trackers that cannot distinguish between its deeper and lighter phases, which is important for gauging sleep quality.

Now, Rossman and his colleagues have gained the clearest picture to date by analysing sleep and dietary data collected from more than 3500 adults, aged 53, on average. “Previous studies haven’t looked at so many dietary and sleep factors,” says Marie-Pierre St-Onge at Columbia University in New York City.

Over two consecutive days, each participant logged what they ate in a mobile app, either during its consumption or shortly afterwards. At night, they wore a device that has been approved by the US Food and Drug Administration to measure sleep. This is composed of sensors worn on the chest, a wrist and a finger that track snoring, blood oxygen levels, and heart and breathing rates.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

The researchers used these measurements to estimate how long the participants spent in the various stages of slumber: light sleep (known as N1 and N2), the deep, restorative phase (N3) and rapid eye movement (REM) sleep, when dreams tend to be long and abstract.

Next, the researchers used a computational model to predict how 25 dietary factors affected sleep that night. They accounted for factors that could skew the results, including age, sex, caffeine intake and – most importantly – dietary and sleep data from the previous day. “Controlling for the previous day really allowed us to explore how what you eat in the day affects your next night of sleep,” says Rossman.

The researchers found that the participants who reported eating more fibre than the cohort’s average of 21 grams per day – equivalent to around 2.5 cups of peas – tended to get better sleep that night than those with a below-average fibre intake. For instance, the former group spent 3.4 per cent more time in N3 and 2.3 per cent less of it in light sleep. “They’re moving in a direction of more restorative sleep, which is important for a healthier brain and body,” says St-Onge.

It is unclear exactly why fibre has these effects, but studies suggest that gut microbes can ferment it into short-chain fatty acids, such as butyrate, that reduce inflammation and alter gut-to-brain signalling in a way that promotes deep sleep, says Rossman.

The researchers also found that those with an above-average fibre intake had a slightly lower heart rate during the night than those who consumed less. A low nocturnal heart rate suggests that a person has entered a state of deep rest and repair, which puts less strain on the heart. “A 1 beat-per-minute difference [as observed between the high- and low-fibre groups] might not be that important for one night, but if that difference is sustained over decades or a lifetime, that could make an important difference [for cardiovascular health],” says St-Onge.

Further analysis revealed that those who ate more than five types of plant-based foods a day also fell asleep slightly faster and had a lower heart rate during sleep than those who ate less. This could be because eating a broader mix of plants provides a range of vitamins, minerals and other molecules, like polyphenols, that help to reduce inflammation and shift the nervous system into a rest-and-digest mode, says Rossman.

Future trials, ideally in sleep clinics, that randomly assign people to eat different amounts of fibre and broader or narrower ranges of plant-based foods are needed to confirm the findings, says St-Onge.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a comprehensive scientific study showing that increased dietary fiber and plant variety improve deep sleep quality and cardiovascular health indicators, which has broad implications for public health and well-being.
articles.personality_title: Eating more fiber and plants helps improve deep sleep and heart health
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Getting a good night's sleep is important for health, but many people struggle with it. Previous studies hinted that eating fiber and plants might help, but they often relied on memory-based surveys or less precise sleep trackers.

**What happened** – Researchers from the Weizmann Institute of Science studied over 3,500 adults around age 53. For two days, participants recorded everything they ate using a mobile app. At night, they wore FDA-approved devices that measured different sleep stages, heart rate, breathing, and snoring. The study looked closely at how 25 different food factors affected sleep that very night.

**Impact** – The study found that people who ate more fiber than average (about 21 grams or 2.5 cups of peas) spent more time in deep, restorative sleep and less time in lighter sleep. They also had lower heart rates during sleep, which suggests better rest and less strain on the heart. Eating a wider variety of plant-based foods helped people fall asleep faster and also lowered heart rates. These findings show that simple changes in diet can improve sleep quality and support heart health.

**What's next step** – Scientists suggest running controlled experiments in sleep clinics where people are assigned different fiber and plant diets to confirm these results. This will help understand exactly how fiber and plant variety improve sleep and heart health.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Eating more fiber and a variety of plant foods can help people sleep more deeply and support heart health overnight.
articles.personality_title_fr: Manger plus de fibres et de plantes améliore le sommeil profond et la santé cardiaque
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Bien dormir est essentiel pour la santé, mais beaucoup de personnes ont des difficultés. Des études précédentes suggéraient que manger des fibres et des plantes pouvait aider, mais elles se basaient souvent sur des souvenirs ou des appareils moins précis.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Institut Weizmann ont étudié plus de 3 500 adultes d'environ 53 ans. Pendant deux jours, les participants ont noté tout ce qu'ils mangeaient dans une application mobile. La nuit, ils portaient des appareils approuvés par la FDA qui mesuraient les différentes phases du sommeil, le rythme cardiaque, la respiration et le ronflement. L'étude a examiné comment 25 facteurs alimentaires influençaient le sommeil cette même nuit.

**Impact** – L'étude a montré que ceux qui mangeaient plus de fibres que la moyenne (environ 21 grammes ou 2,5 tasses de pois) passaient plus de temps en sommeil profond et moins en sommeil léger. Ils avaient aussi un rythme cardiaque plus bas pendant le sommeil, signe d'un meilleur repos et d'une moindre fatigue pour le cœur. Manger une plus grande variété de plantes aidait aussi à s'endormir plus vite et réduisait le rythme cardiaque. Ces résultats montrent que de simples changements alimentaires peuvent améliorer la qualité du sommeil et soutenir la santé cardiaque.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques proposent de mener des expériences contrôlées en cliniques du sommeil où les personnes suivent différents régimes riches ou pauvres en fibres et plantes pour confirmer ces résultats. Cela aidera à comprendre comment les fibres et la variété de plantes améliorent le sommeil et la santé cardiaque.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Manger plus de fibres et une variété d’aliments végétaux aide à mieux dormir profondément et protège la santé du cœur pendant la nuit.
articles.personality_title_es: Comer más fibra y plantas mejora el sueño profundo y la salud del corazón
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Dormir bien es importante para la salud, pero muchas personas tienen problemas para lograrlo. Estudios previos sugirieron que comer fibra y plantas podría ayudar, pero se basaban en recuerdos o en dispositivos menos precisos.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del Instituto Weizmann estudiaron a más de 3,500 adultos con una edad promedio de 53 años. Durante dos días, los participantes registraron todo lo que comieron en una aplicación móvil. Por la noche, usaron dispositivos aprobados por la FDA que midieron las etapas del sueño, ritmo cardíaco, respiración y ronquidos. El estudio analizó cómo 25 factores dietéticos afectaban el sueño esa misma noche.

**Impacto** – El estudio encontró que quienes consumían más fibra que el promedio (unos 21 gramos o 2.5 tazas de guisantes) pasaban más tiempo en sueño profundo y menos en sueño ligero. También tenían un ritmo cardíaco más bajo durante el sueño, lo que indica mejor descanso y menos esfuerzo para el corazón. Comer una mayor variedad de plantas ayudó a dormir más rápido y también redujo el ritmo cardíaco. Estos resultados muestran que cambios simples en la dieta pueden mejorar la calidad del sueño y la salud del corazón.

**Qué sigue** – Los científicos recomiendan hacer estudios controlados en clínicas del sueño donde las personas sigan dietas con diferentes cantidades de fibra y variedad de plantas para confirmar estos hallazgos. Esto ayudará a entender cómo la fibra y la variedad de plantas mejoran el sueño y la salud cardíaca.

**Resumen en una frase** – Comer más fibra y una variedad de plantas ayuda a dormir más profundamente y protege la salud del corazón durante la noche.
articles.image_prompt: A cozy, softly glowing bedroom scene at night where a large, stylized bowl overflowing with diverse colorful fruits, vegetables, nuts, and grains gently transforms into a peaceful, abstract silhouette of a sleeping figure surrounded by swirling, layered waves symbolizing deep, restorative sleep stages, all rendered in warm, natural earth tones and muted greens.
